_meta.description: Seed data for Tokyo in Season — Tokyo Tea Ceremony Price Index. Split into one file per experience under /src/content/experiences/. practitioner_rating: A = Practitioner's pick, B = Good for tourists, C = Skip — with honest reason. editor_note to be written by Ayaka (required before publishing). Fields marked null need verification.
_meta.price_status: Verified: casual-tea-asakusa, hamarikyu, shizu-kokoro, jugetsudo, chazen-ginza, nadeshiko, hisui. Third-party sourced (verify): maikoya x2, chazen-asakusa. Pending: 10 items - see price_note.
_meta.status: FINAL SEED v1 - 19 entries confirmed by editor. Prices marked PENDING to be filled by update-experiences script.

experiences.name: Maikoya Asakusa — Kimono Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: Kimono dressing, private bookings, even nerikiri sweet-making add-ons — the pick if you want a broad cultural bundle rather than tea alone.
experiences.price_note: Approx 2400 tea only / 5200-6000 with kimono per 3rd-party articles (2024-2025). Verify on official page.

experiences.name: Chazen Asakusa
experiences.editor_note: A properly built tea room, complete with tsukubai water basin and nijiriguchi crawl-through entrance — rare among tourist venues. Handles larger groups well.
experiences.price_note: Ginza branch verified at 3500 without kimono; Asakusa assumed same. Verify.

experiences.name: Nadeshiko Tea House
experiences.editor_note: No independent website we could verify — a platform-only operator. Budget-friendly on paper, but we haven't visited and can't vouch beyond the listing.

experiences.name: Shizu-Kokoro Tea Ceremony School
experiences.editor_note: The instructor has taught in the US and runs an actual tea school; this is the closest a visitor gets to a real keiko practice lesson.
experiences.notes: Koicha and usucha both served; guest and host roles; monthly seasonal themes. Priority for a first-hand review visit.

experiences.name: Asakusa Jidaiya Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: Kimono and tea with chairs available, but the space is a studio rather than a tea room — enjoyable, though firmly an 'experience' rather than the real setting.

experiences.name: Casual Tea Ceremony (Tokyo Tourist Lounge Asakusa)
experiences.editor_note: A relaxed, photos-welcome session — honest about being casual rather than formal. Platform-only operator with strong reviews; we haven't visited ourselves.

experiences.name: Private Tea Ceremony at Kobohji Temple (Mita)
experiences.editor_note: The rare option to wear an Oshima Tsumugi silk haori is a lovely touch. Who actually performs the ceremony is unclear — not yet verified first-hand.
experiences.notes: Venue correction by editor: temple is likely Kobohji (弘法寺) in Mita, not Asakusa. Verify address before publishing.

experiences.name: Maikoya Shinjuku — Kimono Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: Same broad bundle as the Asakusa branch — kimono, private options, sweet-making — with the bonus of being walkable from Shinjuku Station.
experiences.price_note: Same pricing structure as Asakusa branch assumed. Verify.

experiences.name: Tea Room Kakoi (Shibuya)
experiences.editor_note: Organic matcha tasting angle is appealing, but this is a platform-only operator we haven't verified first-hand.

experiences.name: Private Tea Ceremony Shibuya (shibuya-travel)
experiences.editor_note: Private 1-4 person format is a plus. Platform-only operator, not independently verified.

experiences.name: Shibuya Authentic Tea Ceremony (50 min)
experiences.editor_note: Platform-only operator, not independently verified — book with expectations set accordingly.

experiences.name: Chazen Ginza (main branch)
experiences.editor_note: A properly built tea room with tsukubai and nijiriguchi, next door to the Kabuki-za theatre — easy to make a full day of traditional culture.

experiences.name: HiSUi TOKYO (Ginza)
experiences.editor_note: A multi-discipline cultural school; hosts take time to explain the seasonal meaning behind each utensil and the spirit of hospitality.

experiences.name: Jugetsudo Tea Ceremony (Kabukiza Tower)
experiences.editor_note: A very light, simplified matcha tasting rather than anything close to a ceremony.
experiences.notes: Kengo Kuma-designed cafe; matcha starter set included as gift

experiences.name: Happo-en Garden Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: A genuinely beautiful garden venue, and the experience can be custom-designed to your group on request.

experiences.name: Urasenke YOUWAKAI Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: Well-known tea practitioners are involved in this school, and the tea room is unusually spacious. It takes a little courage to inquire, but this opens the door to the real depth of the culture.
experiences.notes: Same school as author. Priority for a first-hand review visit; strongest E-E-A-T tie-in on the list.

experiences.name: Ocharu Tea Experience (near Tokyo University / Nezu)
experiences.editor_note: A multi-tea tasting (black tea, gyokuro) rather than a tea ceremony. If matcha ritual is what you came for, look elsewhere — as a tea tasting it may suit you fine.
experiences.notes: Modern multi-tea tasting (wakocha, gyokuro) rather than a formal ceremony.

experiences.name: Fukagawa Geisha Tea Ceremony
experiences.editor_note: Reviews are weak, and the atmosphere is far from a formal tea gathering.

experiences.name: Hamarikyu Gardens — Nakajima-no-Ochaya
experiences.editor_note: This is a café, not a ceremony — which makes it perfect if a full tea ceremony feels like more commitment than you want.
experiences.notes: Matcha and wagashi in a garden teahouse; entry-level 'tea moment' rather than a ceremony.
experiences.price_note: Matcha + seasonal wagashi set 850 yen, plus garden entry 300 yen. Verify current status before publishing.
